name: HTTP Request Handler
description: Exposes an HTTP endpoint that accepts JSON payloads, validates the input, processes the data, and returns a JSON response. Demonstrates the Request/Response pattern in Power Automate.
key_features: HTTP POST trigger with JSON schema validation

flow_steps.action: Trigger: When an HTTP request is received
flow_steps.description: Exposes an HTTPS endpoint. Power Automate generates the URL after saving the flow.
Creates a unique ticket ID using: concat('TKT-', formatDateTime(utcNow(), 'yyyyMMdd'), '-', rand(1000, 9999))

flow_steps.action: Condition: Check if required fields are present
flow_steps.true_branch: Process the request and create ticket

flow_steps.description: Logs the request to a SharePoint list for tracking.
Sends a confirmation to the requester with their ticket ID.
Returns a 200 OK with the created ticket details as JSON.

response_body.message: Your request has been received. You will receive an email confirmation shortly.
response_body.estimatedResponseTime: @{if(equals(triggerBody()?['priority'], 'Critical'), '2 hours', if(equals(triggerBody()?['priority'], 'High'), '4 hours', if(equals(triggerBody()?['priority'], 'Medium'), '1 business day', '3 business days')))}

expressions_used: formatDateTime() - Format the date portion of the ticket ID
rand() - Generate a random number for the ticket ID
if() / equals() - Conditional logic for estimated response time
notes: The HTTP trigger URL is generated by Power Automate after the flow is saved.
The HTTP connector is a premium connector and requires a paid Power Automate license.
Use SAS token authentication or an API Management gateway to secure the endpoint.
The generated URL contains a SAS token for basic security but consider adding additional validation.
Test with a tool like Postman or curl: POST to the trigger URL with a JSON body.
